title: Monocular scotoma: lower retina lesion (superior field defect)
vignette: A 60-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up appointment. The patient underwent coronary catheterization 2 weeks ago due to anginal symptoms and had stenting of the left circumflex artery. He has had no chest pain but reports a dark spot in the upper part of his vision since the procedure. The spot disappears when the patient covers the right eye. Medical history also includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, visual acuity is normal bilaterally. Computerized visual field testing shows an isolated dark spot in the superior visual field of the right eye only, with a normal left eye field. Occlusion of an artery supplying which of the following structures is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

clues.note: Arterial catheterization is a source of embolic debris (cholesterol/plaque). A retinal artery branch embolus after a recent cath is the mechanism of this acute monocular visual defect.

clues.text: dark spot in the upper part of his vision
clues.note: A SUPERIOR visual-field defect localizes to the INFERIOR retina (the retina inverts the image). Upper-field loss = lower-retina lesion - key to choosing 'lower retina.'

clues.text: The spot disappears when the patient covers the right eye
clues.note: If covering the right eye abolishes the defect, the defect is seen by the RIGHT eye -> it's monocular (one eye only). Monocular loss localizes ANTERIOR to the optic chiasm: retina or optic nerve, not the brain.

clues.text: isolated dark spot in the superior visual field of the right eye only
clues.note: A monocular scotoma in the superior field of the right eye = a focal lesion of the right INFERIOR retina (branch retinal artery occlusion). This pins both the eye and the retinal quadrant.

whatMatters: Two localizing facts: the defect is monocular (covering the right eye removes it -> right eye only -> lesion anterior to chiasm), and it's in the SUPERIOR field (-> INFERIOR/lower retina, because the retina inverts images). A branch retinal artery occlusion of the lower right retina fits - answer D.
strategy: Localize visual defects in two moves: (1) monocular vs binocular - monocular = retina/optic nerve (anterior to chiasm); binocular/homonymous = optic tract or behind. (2) Field-to-retina inversion - a SUPERIOR field defect = INFERIOR retina; nasal field = temporal retina. Apply both before reading options.

eliminations.label: left occipital lobe - would be binocular/homonymous
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: left occipital (visual cortex) lesions cause a RIGHT homonymous hemianopia (often with macular sparing). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the defect is monocular (vanishes when the right eye is covered), so it must be anterior to the chiasm, not the cortex. You chose this. ✅ Correct if: a binocular right-sided field loss affecting BOTH eyes were described.

eliminations.label: left parietal lobe - binocular inferior quadrantanopia
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: parietal (optic radiation, dorsal) lesions cause a contralateral inferior homonymous quadrantanopia ('pie on the floor'). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is binocular/homonymous; this defect is monocular. ✅ Correct if: both eyes showed a matching inferior quadrant loss.

eliminations.label: left temporal lobe - binocular superior quadrantanopia
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: temporal (Meyer loop) lesions cause a contralateral superior homonymous quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky'). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: homonymous = binocular; the patient's loss is in one eye only. ✅ Correct if: a binocular superior quadrant defect were present.

eliminations.label: right lateral geniculate nucleus - binocular homonymous
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: LGN lesions produce a contralateral homonymous hemianopia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this is post-chiasmal and binocular, not a single-eye scotoma. ✅ Correct if: a left homonymous hemianopia affecting both eyes were described.

eliminations.label: upper right retina - wrong retinal quadrant
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a lesion of the SUPERIOR retina. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the upper retina maps to the INFERIOR visual field; this patient's defect is in the SUPERIOR field, which corresponds to the LOWER retina. ✅ Correct if: the field defect were in the inferior visual field.

reasoning: Covering the right eye removes the spot -> monocular -> lesion anterior to the chiasm (retina/optic nerve)
Superior field defect -> inferior (lower) retina due to image inversion
Embolic source from recent coronary catheterization -> branch retinal artery occlusion
mechanism: Light from the superior visual field strikes the inferior retina and vice versa, so visual-field defects map to the opposite retinal quadrant. A monocular field defect localizes anterior to the optic chiasm (retina or optic nerve), because each eye's input has not yet crossed. Here a recent coronary catheterization is the embolic source for a branch retinal artery occlusion; an embolus to a vessel supplying the right inferior retina produces a monocular SUPERIOR field scotoma. Post-chiasmal lesions (optic tract, LGN, optic radiations, occipital cortex) instead cause binocular, homonymous defects.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neurology > Visual field defects: monocular (retina/optic nerve, anterior to chiasm) vs binocular homonymous (tract/LGN/radiations/cortex); retina-field inversion (superior field <-> inferior retina). Meyer loop (temporal) = superior quadrantanopia; parietal = inferior quadrantanopia.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Decode the two facts the stem hands you: 'disappears when right eye covered' = monocular; 'upper part of vision' = inferior retina. Localize before scanning the cortical options.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on scotoma, monocular vs homonymous, optic chiasm crossing, retina-field inversion, branch retinal artery occlusion, LGN/Meyer loop? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: You chose A (left occipital lobe) - that gives a BINOCULAR right homonymous hemianopia. The cover-test detail proves the defect is in ONE eye, forcing a retinal/optic-nerve answer.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The field diagram shows a spot in the right eye's superior field with a normal left eye. Translate: one eye = monocular; upper field = lower retina.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Train the reflex: cover-test first (mono vs bino), then invert field-to-retina. Two steps, every time.

keywords.back: Anterior to the optic chiasm (retina or optic nerve)

keywords.front: Superior visual field defect = which retina?
Embolus after arterial catheterization causing monocular scotoma

keywords.back: Contralateral superior quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky')
Contralateral inferior quadrantanopia ('pie on the floor')

firstAid.detail: Neuro/Ophtho: monocular visual loss, retina-field inversion, branch retinal artery occlusion.
firstAid.location: Neuro/Ophtho: monocular visual loss, retina-field inversion, branch retinal artery occlusion.

firstAid.detail: Neuro: chiasm, optic tract, LGN, Meyer loop, optic radiations, occipital cortex and their field defects.
firstAid.location: Neuro: chiasm, optic tract, LGN, Meyer loop, optic radiations, occipital cortex and their field defects.

firstAid.detail: CV: cardiac catheterization/atherosclerotic emboli, Hollenhorst plaque, amaurosis fugax.
firstAid.location: CV: cardiac catheterization/atherosclerotic emboli, Hollenhorst plaque, amaurosis fugax.

memoryHook: Cover the eye to find the eye; flip top-for-bottom for the retina. Upper-field spot in one eye = LOWER retina of that eye.
